title: Tight contact structures on Seifert fibered 3-manifolds
speaker: András Stipsicz
abstract: Workshop on Flows, Foliations and Contact Structures 2015-2016 Monday, December 7, 2015 - 08:00 to Friday, December 11, 2015 - 12:00 This workshop is part of the topical program "Geometric Structures on 3-Manifolds" which will take place during the 2015-2016 academic year at the Institute for Advanced Study. This workshop will focus on the relations between flows, 2-dimensional foliations, and contact structures on 3-manifolds.
tags: Workshop on Flows Foliations and Contact Structures

proof_stages.label: Reduction to the atoroidal Seifert-fibered problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the classification of tight contact structures, reduces attention to prime manifolds, contrasts toroidal and atoroidal behavior, and chooses the Seifert-fibered atoroidal side as the target.

proof_stages.label: Lens-space surgery paradigm
proof_stages.summary: The known classification for S3 and lens spaces is reviewed as a model: continued-fraction surgery diagrams are Legendrianized, stabilizations reduce surgeries to contact ±1 cases with unique tight solid-torus extensions, and Stein Chern classes or induced spin-c structures distinguish the resulting contact structures.

proof_stages.label: Seifert plumbing setup and solved e0 regimes
proof_stages.summary: The Seifert spaces Y(e0; r1,r2,r3) are encoded by star-shaped plumbing diagrams and associated four-manifolds, after which the already classified cases e0≤-2 and e0≥0 are explained via adaptations of the lens-space handlebody construction.

proof_stages.label: Exceptional e0=-1 phenomena and L-space restriction
proof_stages.summary: The remaining e0=-1 case is shown to contain nonexistence, nonfillability, and mixed-fillability phenomena, so the talk restricts to the L-space subcase, equivalently described by absence of taut/transverse foliations or by a numerical inequality.

proof_stages.label: Finite candidate diagrams for tight structures
proof_stages.summary: For L-space Seifert manifolds Y(-1; r1,r2,r3), every tight contact structure is shown to appear among a finite family of contact surgery diagrams with two +1 surgeries and negative rational surgeries expanded into continued fractions.

proof_stages.label: Matković classification criterion
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that a candidate diagram is tight exactly when d3(ξ)=d(tξ), and that two tight structures from such diagrams are isotopic exactly when their induced spin-c structures agree.

proof_stages.label: Diagrammatic computation of d3 and d
proof_stages.summary: The d3 invariant is computed from the surgery cobordism using rotation numbers and a Chern-class formula adjusted for +1 surgeries, while the Heegaard Floer correction term d is computed from the induced spin-c structure determined by the same cohomology data.

proof_stages.label: Heegaard Floer full-path proof of tightness and isotopy
proof_stages.summary: By gluing the plumbing to its complementary negative-definite plumbing inside a blown-up rational surface, Ozsváth–Szabó full paths detect the relevant nonzero Heegaard Floer elements; the grading comparison gives d3=d and nonvanishing of the contact invariant, while the full-path moves are promoted to isotopies between diagrams.

examples.name: No-positive-tight Seifert family
examples.note: The family Y(-1; 1/2, n/(2n+1), 1/(2n+3)) shows that the e0=-1 case cannot be handled by the earlier Stein-surgery classification and motivates the need for a refined criterion.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes down the one-parameter Seifert family and states it has no positive tight structures, but does not run a calculation or proof on the family in the transcript

passes.reason: The infinite Seifert family Y(-1;1/2,n/(2n+1),1/(2n+3)) is used to show that positive tight contact structures need not exist.
The family Y(-1;1/2,n/(2n+1),1/(2n+3)) realizes the minimal zero count of positive tight structures and is used to refute general positive-tight existence.
The family Y(-1;1/2,n/(2n+1),1/(2n+3)) is an infinite family of otherwise ordinary small Seifert spaces used to show positive tight contact structures need not exist.
A systematic one-parameter Seifert family, used to show that positive tight contact structures need not exist in this class.
A one-parameter Seifert family used to show that positive tight contact structures need not exist on all such oriented Seifert manifolds.
this infinite Seifert family realizes the minimum possible number, zero, of positive tight contact structures and is used to defeat general existence expectations
The family Y(-1;1/2,n/(2n+1),1/(2n+3)) is used as an obstruction to general positive tight existence and has the minimum possible number, zero, of positive tight contact structures.
the family is used to show positive tight contact structures need not exist, and it realizes the minimum possible count, namely zero, of positive tight structures.
the one-parameter small Seifert family is used to show positive tight contact structures need not exist, but it is a systematic nondegenerate family rather than a sporadic or extremal object
The family is used to show positive tight structures need not exist, and it attains the minimum possible count of such structures, namely zero.
This one-parameter Seifert family is used to show positive tight contact structures need not exist, and it is extremal in having the minimum possible number, namely none.
The family Y(-1,1/2,n/(2n+1),1/(2n+3)) is used to refute universal positive tight existence, and it is extremal in having the minimum possible number—zero—of positive tight contact structures.

examples.name: Legendrian trefoil plus-one surgery
examples.note: A +1 contact surgery on a Legendrian trefoil gives a tight but nonfillable contact structure, illustrating the fillability obstructions that appear in the exceptional case.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the plus-one surgery on a Legendrian trefoil by sketching the nonfillability argument using the L-space property, negative definiteness, a concave filling with form -E7, and Donaldson’s theorem

passes.reason: Contact +1 surgery on a Legendrian trefoil gives a tight but non-fillable contact manifold, refuting any naive tight-implies-fillable expectation.
Contact +1 surgery on a Legendrian trefoil gives a tight but nonfillable contact manifold, used against any tight-implies-fillable expectation.
Contact +1 surgery on a Legendrian trefoil gives a tight but nonfillable contact manifold, refuting any expectation that tightness implies fillability.
A standard contact-surgery example on a trefoil, used to exhibit a tight contact structure that is not fillable.
The contact manifold obtained by +1 surgery on a Legendrian trefoil is used as a tight but non-fillable example, refuting tight-implies-fillable expectations.
the plus-one surgery on a Legendrian trefoil is structurally an ordinary contact-surgery example, used to show that a tight contact structure need not be fillable

The +1 contact surgery on a Legendrian trefoil is cited as a tight but nonfillable contact manifold, refuting any tight-implies-fillable expectation, but it is not structurally sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric.

contact +1 surgery on a Legendrian trefoil is used to exhibit a tight but nonfillable contact structure, refuting any tight-implies-fillable expectation, while the object is not intrinsically sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric.

plus-one surgery on a Legendrian trefoil is presented as a tight but nonfillable contact example, refuting any tight-implies-fillable expectation, with no intrinsic maximal symmetry or classification-exception status

The contact +1 surgery on a Legendrian trefoil is used as a tight but nonfillable example, refuting any implication from tightness to fillability, while the object is otherwise not structurally sporadic or degenerate.
Contact +1 surgery on a Legendrian trefoil is presented as a tight but nonfillable example, refuting any implication that tightness entails fillability.

examples.name: Same-family Seifert manifolds with mixed fillability
examples.note: A family of Seifert fibered spaces is cited as carrying both Stein-fillable and nonfillable tight contact structures, showing that topology alone does not separate the possible tight structures.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker states that some Seifert manifolds in the same family have both Stein-fillable and nonfillable tight structures, but gives no concrete construction or verification for those examples

passes.reason: The Seifert manifolds with both Stein-fillable and non-fillable tight structures show that fillability is not determined solely by the underlying family/manifold.
These ordinary Seifert-family manifolds are used to show fillability is not determined just by the underlying topology, since tight structures on them can be Stein fillable or nonfillable.
These ordinary Seifert fibered examples with both Stein-fillable and nonfillable tight structures show fillability is not determined just by the underlying Seifert family or topology.
A systematic Seifert-family phenomenon, used to show that fillability is not determined just by the underlying 3-manifold family.
These Seifert manifolds are used to show that even within the same topological family one can have both Stein-fillable and non-fillable tight structures.
the same-family Seifert manifolds are not structural classification exceptions, but are used to refute the expectation that fillability is uniform or purely topological for their tight structures
